source: The Forever Winter shipping game files (build 24045295) — RandomContainerLootData + the placed BP_LootContainer_* crate blueprints + BP_CrateSpawner_ForQuests, decoded with CUE4Parse.
generated_note: How the drop system actually works — placement, tiers, contents — curated to lead with the mechanic. The datamined numbers (tier budgets, item caps, quest spawn rates) are regenerated by tools/parse_crate_types.py as a diff reference.
tldr: A crate's **type** and **tier** are fixed to its spot on the map; only its **contents** roll. Tier is a **credit budget**, not better odds. The only crates that roll whether they show up at all are **quest crates**. And a kill is not a drop — what you shot leaves a **separate container you still have to search**.

model.title: Kills — the wreck is a container, and you have to open it
model.lead: Killing a turret, drone, mech or soldier puts **nothing** in your inventory. It spawns a **searchable container** where the thing died — and if you walk away, you get zero, forever.
model.body: Every AI unit carries a **Loot Object** (`BP_RandomLoot_*`), and destroying the unit spawns it as a container you must physically walk to and search. That container has its own name — the one you'll see on screen — and it is almost never the enemy's name: a Europan Turret leaves **Turret Debris**, an Exo leaves **Exo Debris**, a Pyro leaves **Pyro's Bag**. Those debris names are what this tab lists as sources, because that's the object you actually open. Two traps live here. **One:** unlike a crate, an entity pool's budget floor is **0** — a legitimate roll can leave the container empty, so a kill is never a guaranteed payout. **Two:** the caps are tight — a turret draws **at most 3** items from a 23-item pool, a Cyborg exactly **1** — so a headline pool share is spread across very few pulls. If you're farming one specific component, expect to open a lot of debris.

model.title: Placement — the type is welded to the spot
model.lead: Every crate you see is a specific placed object, and its type never changes. A Materials Bin is always a Materials Bin, in the same corner, every raid.
model.body: In the files each crate is its own blueprint — `BP_LootContainer_Material_T2`, `…_Army_T4`, and so on — with the container type *and* its loot pool baked in. Designers place those blueprints by hand, so a spot's **type and tier are fixed, not rolled**. The single exception is quest crates, which genuinely roll whether they spawn (see below).

model.title: Tier — a credit budget, not better odds
model.lead: A crate's tier — the **A / B / C / D** on its label — is a spending limit on how much loot *value* it may hold: T1 50k → T2 100k → T3 150k → T4 200k credits.
model.body: Every tier of a type draws from the **same item pool**; the higher tier just has a bigger budget to fill, so it packs more — and pricier — pulls before it stops. A crate keeps adding weighted items until it either spends its budget or hits its **item cap** (Materials 9, most families 6, Armaments & Weapon-Mods 3). So a Military 'D' isn't rolling from a better table than an 'A' — it just carries 4× the wallet.

model.title: Contents — re-rolled every single open
model.lead: What's actually inside is rolled fresh each time from that type's fixed pool. Two identical crates rarely hold the same thing.
model.body: Each item's **pool share** (shown on every source below) is its weight within one draw — relative scarcity when you crack *this* crate, **not** a per-raid probability. Rarity is measured **per pool**: cheap filler can read *Ultra Rare* simply because it's an unlikely pull, and the same item reads differently under two crate types. Always read a rarity together with the crate it sits under.

tierNote: MaxValue per tier from RandomContainerLootData — the credit ceiling a crate of that tier fills toward. MinValue is 0, so a low roll can come up nearly empty.

crateTypes.inside: Weapon parts, military tape & cables, gas masks, PDAs, high-frequency radios — militaria and hardware.
Raw salvage & manufacturing stock — planks, wires, circuit boards, relays, gauges. The broadest pool, and the fattest cap (9).
Tourniquets, syringes, first-aid kits, bandages, blood packs — a tight, reliable medical pool.
Food, drink and tobacco — food tins, chips, beer, spirits, cigarettes. Cheap but dense.
Civilian tools & electronics — tool kits, wrenches, screwdrivers, USB & MD drives, matches.
Pure drink for resale — vodka, gin, rum, absinthe, mead. Small pool, all sell fodder.
Ammunition & grenades — 9mm, buckshot, rifle rounds, frags. Contents live on the Ammo tab, so they're not re-listed here.
Weapon attachments — sights, lasers, flashlights, muzzle devices. Contents live on the Weapons/Attachments tabs.

entityNote: What each killed unit leaves behind, read from its Loot Object's `RandomLoot.RowName` + `ContainerDetails.ContainerName`. **Cap** = most items the wreck can hold; every one of these pools has a budget floor of **0**, so any of them can come up empty. Note the game keeps *orphaned* pre-EconV2 loot rows in the same table — `RookLoot_Turret_Europa` still reads 48.85% Turret Component — but nothing references them; the live rows below are the ones the engine actually rolls.

entityDrops.name: Turret Debris
entityDrops.note: Every turret in the game — Europan, 20mm, Scav, Water Thief — rolls this one pool.

entityDrops.name: Europan Drone Debris
entityDrops.note: The flight controller is the prize; IFF chips ride along.

entityDrops.name: Exo Debris
entityDrops.note: Exo Component, plus heavy machine salvage.

entityDrops.name: Medium Mech Debris
entityDrops.note: Europa and Euruska med-mechs share it.

entityDrops.name: T-90 Debris
entityDrops.note: Tank hull — deep pool, roomy budget.

entityDrops.name: Merkava Debris
entityDrops.note: Tank hull — deep pool, roomy budget.

entityDrops.name: Cyborg Remains
entityDrops.note: Caps at a single item — one pull and it's done.

questSpawnNote: Quest crates are the only drops with a real spawn roll. `BP_CrateSpawner_ForQuests` checks whether you're on its quest, then rolls its **Chance to spawn** — which is why maps mark *possible* crate locations and only a subset appears each raid.

questSpawn.note: Placed for its quest — always appears once you're carrying it.
Only one in three — expect to re-run the raid for it.

special.name: High-probability caches
special.body: Hand-placed richer crates that skip the budget model for a **guaranteed-good** pull: **Medical Supplies** (always ~10.5k–14k cr), **Weapon Mods**, **Ammunition**, and an **AA-gun component** cache. Rare on the map, worth the detour.

special.name: Level ‘Supplies’ crates
special.body: Generic **Supplies / Storage** crates pull from a **per-map** pool (City, Ashen Mesa, Frozen Swamp, Shanti) with a 20k–32k budget — the catch-all placed where a spot isn't one of the typed families.

special.name: Boss Codex drills
special.body: A boss's **Codex** isn't a crate — you drill it out of the corpse (or grab a placed one), and each boss has its own `CodexLoot` pool. Putting the boss down is the hard part — see the **Enemies** tab for the stun-and-DetPack method.

special.name: World rare-loot
special.body: A separate overlay (`BP_RareLootManager`) sprinkles a rare-item roll with its *own* drop chance into containers, independent of the crate's pool — the occasional jackpot that doesn't belong to the type. Listed under **World Rare-Loot** below.
